perturbation.original_text: Schoolpop shall comply with all applicable Federal, State and local laws concerning the use of Cards, including anti-money laundering and anti-terrorism laws, and currency control regulations. Schoolpop warrants it shall take measures to track information about its Clients which shall meet compliance requirements related to the above stated laws, track all Card shipment(s) to each such Client, and maintain appropriate records for its Clients for a period of seven (7) years after termination of the Agreement and to provide such records and other information to AEIS upon reasonable request and subject to applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Schoolpop shall comply with some Federal laws concerning the use of Cards, maybe anti-money laundering and anti-terrorism laws, and possibly currency control regulations. Schoolpop warrants it shall take measures to track information about some of its Clients which may or may not meet compliance requirements related to the above stated laws, maybe track all Card shipment(s) to some of each such Client, and possibly maintain records for a period of three (3) years after termination of the Agreement and to provide such records and other information to AEIS at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces legal uncertainty by changing 'all applicable Federal, State and local laws' to 'some Federal laws' and decreasing the maintenance of records from seven years to three, making compliance vague and discretionary. Additionally, it makes providing records to AEIS conditional on AEIS's 'discretion'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 31 U.S. Code § 5318 - Compliance programs and reports
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts 31 U.S. Code § 5318, which mandates financial institutions to establish and maintain compliance programs, including record-keeping and reporting requirements, to prevent money laundering. By making compliance optional and shortening the record retention period, the modified text undermines the legal obligation to maintain a robust anti-money laundering program.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces legal uncertainty and contradicts the obligation to comply with anti-money laundering laws. The original text mandates compliance with 'all applicable Federal, State and local laws,' while the changed text weakens this to 'some Federal laws,' introducing ambiguity. The duration of record maintenance is also reduced from seven to three years and subject to AEIS's discretion, undermining the obligation to maintain a robust anti-money laundering program as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing 31 U.S. Code § 5318. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 31 USC 5318, indicating its relevance to compliance, exemptions, and summons authority, further solidifying the link to anti-money laundering compliance.

perturbation.original_text: AEIS may from time to time provide Schoolpop with certain reasonable terms and provisions to be included in the agreements to be entered into between Schoolpop and its Clients and Schoolpop agrees that any such terms and provisions shall be included in such agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: AEIS may from time to time provide Schoolpop with suggestions to be included in the agreements to be entered into between Schoolpop and its Clients and Schoolpop may consider including those suggestions in such agreements.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the mandatory obligation ('agrees that any such terms and provisions shall be included') with a discretionary action ('may consider including those suggestions'). This introduces a contradiction by giving Schoolpop the freedom to ignore AEIS's terms and provisions, weakening AEIS's control over the downstream agreements and introducing vagueness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the inclusion of AEIS's terms optional, the modification may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, as codified in UCC § 1-304. The duty of good faith requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of their contractual obligations. By allowing Schoolpop to disregard AEIS's terms, the modification enables Schoolpop to potentially act in bad faith, undermining the contractual relationship and the expected benefits for AEIS.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens AEIS's control by changing 'agrees that any such terms and provisions shall be included' to 'may consider including those suggestions.' This introduces a legal contradiction as it undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. UCC § 1-304 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By making the inclusion of AEIS's terms optional, Schoolpop could potentially act in bad faith, contradicting this obligation. The original text ensures AEIS's terms are included, while the changed text allows Schoolpop to disregard them.

perturbation.original_text: Schoolpop acknowledges that Points must be used prior to the expiration date of the Cards. No refund will be made with respect to Points remaining on Cards at the time of expiration to Schoolpop, its Clients or the Cardholders, subject to applicable law. Schoolpop further acknowledges the Cardholder Agreement contains such notice to Cardholders.
perturbation.changed_text: Schoolpop acknowledges that Points should be used prior to the expiration date of the Cards. Refunds may be available with respect to Points remaining on Cards at the time of expiration to Schoolpop, its Clients or the Cardholders, except where prohibited by specific agreement. Schoolpop confirms the Cardholder Agreement may or may not contain such notice to Cardholders.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the enforceability of the expiration date and introduce uncertainty about refunds and notification. By changing "must be used" to "should be used" and introducing potential refunds, the clause contradicts the clear intent of the original text. Furthermore, by saying the Cardholder Agreement may or may not contain such notice to Cardholders, there is a possibility that parties who are not fully aware of the terms of usage could be affected.
perturbation.law_explanation: By introducing the possibility of refunds for expired points, the modified text may conflict with state escheat laws regarding unclaimed property. Many states, including Delaware (where Schoolpop is incorporated), have laws requiring companies to report and remit unclaimed property, including unused gift card balances, to the state after a certain dormancy period. If the contract allows refunds, it needs to comply with these escheat laws, but the vague wording creates uncertainty about whether the company is fulfilling its obligations under those laws, especially in regards to unused balances, creating a contradiction if the balance has not been remitted according to the dormancy laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 12 > Chapter 11 Authenticated PDF TITLE 12 Decedentsâ Estates and Fiduciary Relations Descent and Distribution; Escheat CHAPTER 11. Escheats Subchapter III. Unclaimed Life Insurance Funds
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces the possibility of refunds for expired points, which contradicts escheat laws. The Law Explanation states that many states, including Delaware, have laws requiring companies to report and remit unclaimed property, including unused gift card balances, to the state after a certain dormancy period. The original text states "No refund will be made with respect to Points remaining on Cards at the time of expiration". The modified text changes this to "Refunds may be available with respect to Points remaining on Cards at the time of expiration". This creates a potential conflict with escheat laws if the company does not remit the unused balances according to state law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Delaware's escheat laws under Title 12, Chapter 11, indicating that such laws exist and could be violated by the possibility of refunds without adherence to escheat regulations.
